coredata.dc:description: The widespread efficacy of graphene in nanotechnology has allowed researchers over the years to merge its unique features with quantum confinement effects to unlock newer possibilities in multiple domains. In this work, we have tailored a two-dimensional graphene sheet to 8 distinctive achiral quantum dots and systematically explored their tunable electro-optical responses through surface functionalization with consequent applicability in gas sensing. Emergent traces of spin-splitting in shape-dependent zigzag edges highlight the possibility of metal-free magnetic behavior of graphene in ambient conditions. Absorption and fluorescence spectra have been critically explored with a diverse range of oscillator strengths in the presence of water as a solvent (TD-DFT/CAM-B3LYP/PCM model) revealing the underlying alluring optical signatures. The introduced charge anisotropy through doping of III-V group elements caters to significant dipole moments that attract health-hazardous polar gas molecules like carbon monoxide (CO) with strengthened sensitivity.

head.ce:title: Functionalized graphene quantum dots with distinctive solvent-driven emission and enhanced carbon monoxide sensing: A DFT study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Calcutta, 92 A. P. C. Road, Kolkata 700009, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, University of Calcutta

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread efficacy of graphene in nanotechnology has allowed researchers over the years to merge its unique features with quantum confinement effects to unlock newer possibilities in multiple domains. In this work, we have tailored a two-dimensional graphene sheet to 8 distinctive achiral quantum dots and systematically explored their tunable electro-optical responses through surface functionalization with consequent applicability in gas sensing. Emergent traces of spin-splitting in shape-dependent zigzag edges highlight the possibility of metal-free magnetic behavior of graphene in ambient conditions. Absorption and fluorescence spectra have been critically explored with a diverse range of oscillator strengths in the presence of water as a solvent (TD-DFT/CAM-B3LYP/PCM model) revealing the underlying alluring optical signatures. The introduced charge anisotropy through doping of III-V group elements caters to significant dipole moments that attract health-hazardous polar gas molecules like carbon monoxide (CO) with strengthened sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Among various elements used for diverse scientific development, carbon (C) has grabbed the attention of current researchers due to its excellent hybridization capability [1]. For this catenation property, different carbon allotropes have been synthesized, amidst which graphene is the most fascinating 2D material of nanoscience and technology. In 2004, after the discovery [2] of extremely thin monolayer of carbon atoms containing sp
2
hybridized bond with a bond length of 0.142 nm, graphene has pioneered significant advances in physics and chemistry. Experimentally, graphene films have also been synthesized via several methods [3,4]. These materials are, however, non-toxic in nature, having vast surface area per unit mass, excellent carrier mobility, stable photo-luminescence and good thermal and chemical stability [5]. Two most important properties include unusual linear energy spectrum and massless nature of charge carriers [6,7] which ensure the application of graphene in flexible transistors, sensors and batteries [8]. However, due to its zero band gap [9], there is restriction of its usage in electronic circuit devices. Although, the reduction of graphene’s dimension introduces energy gap through the distortion of lattice symmetry by means of chemical functionalization [10] and by doping with foreign atoms in pure graphene [11–13]. In the year 2010, Balog et al. experimentally generated the energy gap in graphene through nanopatterning technique [14]. When lateral dimensions of graphene sheets are reduced to the nanoscale by passivating dangling bonds with hydrogen atoms, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) emerge [15]. GQD behaves like an ‘artificial atom’ because of the confinement of electronic motion in all three spatial directions [16]. P. Rani et al. [17] theoretically have confirmed that the tuning of energy gap can be achieved by customizing the size of the structures and passivating with hydrogen (H) and fluorine (F) atoms. Their results reveal that different shape and morphology of GQD can tune the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) - lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) gap in particular C24H12 (4.08 eV), C42H18 (3.61 eV) and C54H18 (2.85 eV). Besides, electronic properties can effectively be altered utilizing heteroatom doping in different morphology of GQDs with synergistic effect. The enhancement of optoelectronic traits through single atom doping such as nitrogen (N) [18], sulfur (S) [19], boron (B) [20], phosphorus (P) [21], chlorine (Cl) [22] and oxygen (O) [23] have been probed. Further, compared with single heteroatom doped GQDs, large heterogeneity of physicochemical properties can be perceived using dual heteroatoms doped GQDs. Feng et al. highlight that B/P/S doping introduces new optoelectronic characteristics to GQDs [24]. A recent article published by Ajeel et al. [25] showcases prominent study of three arrangements of BN dimers (Ortho, Meta, Para) doped GQDs. They proposed an important fact that BN dimers transform the insulator to a semiconductor. Moreover, graphene nanostructures display another remarkable property of magnetism which originates due to their open-shell electronic structure that can be executed by (i) edge magnetism [26] (ii) topological frustration [27] (iii) sublattice imbalance [28] and (iv) topological states at the interfaces between different graphene nanoribbons or quantum dots [29]. A handful of works suggest modification of magnetic properties by doping [30], heterostructure construction [31] or by provoking graphene nanostructures [32]. This feature makes GQD as a key member in spintronic devices [33,34].

To explore the chemical fingerprint, Raman spectroscopy is investigated as a characterization technique. Bandyopadhyay et al. [35] observed vibrational modes in pristine and doped GQDs. They have identified a prominent phonon Raman line ∼ 1711 cm−1 related to CC stretching mode. In 2018, Chakraborti et al. established that Stone–Wales defect mode introduces the red shift of G mode after the incorporation of defects in GQDs. UV-visible spectra analysis, being a promising method to predict the optical properties of GQDs, can explain the characteristics of ultra-violet (UV) absorption, emission and photoluminescence. Ozonder and collaborators have explored the UV–Vis absorption spectra of 63 square-shaped GQDs by time dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculation [36]. The results reveal that the shift in the peaks of UV and visible parts of the spectrum are due to the variation in their sizes, dopant type, and dopant percentage. The UV absorption spectra of GQDs is measured in the range 200 to 900 nm [37]. Two distinct peaks are obvious [38] at 240.5 nm and 350 nm, respectively denote the band to band ( π -
π
∗
) and defect state to band (n-
π
∗
) transitions. Prominent peak positions corresponding to π -
π
∗
are blue-shifted due to decrease in the size of the quantum dots. GQDs are almost soluble in distinct solvents like water, ethanol, acetic acid. The effect of these solvents in the absorption spectra of C54H18 and C54H22 are investigated by Shtepliuk et al. [39]. GQDs can produce two photons in a single excitation event, which is responsible for the emission of coherent light, makes it efficient in production of laser. This extraordinary property approaches various implementations such as sensors [40], light conversion, photo catalysis, bio imaging and photovoltaics. Zhu et al. presented an overall review article on the application of GQDs in the field of chemiresistive gas sensing [41]. In a theoretical study, another health-hazardous gas (carbon monoxide) was sensed in BN codoped T-graphene structure with recovery time of 12.80 ps [42]. A recent work in 2025 by Das et al. [43] focuses on the Machine Learning (ML) technique used to detect metal ions in Brahmaputra River water where they have trained various ML models with the dataset generated from plasmonic photodetectors constructed using in-house synthesized Au@N-GQDs heterostructures.

With this motivation, we would like to systematically explore the variation in the electronic, magnetic and optical properties of distinctive GQDs along with their possible III-V doped variants. Our center of attention focuses on the fact that we can detect the magnetism by monitoring the structure physically without scrutinizing its morphology through computer simulation. In Raman spectra analysis, along with the study of basic vibrational modes, another interesting alloy stretching mode is identified in doped structures. A detailed literature survey however suggests absorption peaks of selected GQDs with particular shape, size and dopant type; consequently, a complete organized study of fluorescence spectra with water as a solvent is currently lacking. Instead of the remarkable amount of attention spent on this genre, there are still voids to be filled and features left to interpret. Therefore, the objective of our work is to concentrate on the thorough inspection of the absorption and emission of all the structures along with their doped derivatives.

This paper is organized as follows. In the next section, we have discussed the methodology for computational details and the formulae used for important calculations. Thereafter, next few subsections consist of critical discussions of the structural and electronic properties, Raman and UV–Vis spectra analysis and gas sensing applications. Finally, conclusions are drawn for our work.

We have studied the geometrical and optical properties of all derived GQDs under the scheme of density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in Gaussian 09 software [44,45]. The self-consistent ground state has been achieved using Becke’s three-parameter exchange functional with the Lee–Yang–Parr correlation functional (B3LYP) combined with the basis set 6-31G(d) [46]. All the structures have been optimized to global minima by the relaxation under the threshold value of the maximum force per atom i.e. 0.000450 Hartrees/Bohr with no imaginary frequency. To establish the structural stability, the cohesive energy per atom (

Moreover, for the characterization of all the optimized structures, the first order Raman spectra have been computed. A scaling factor of 0.9614 is introduced for our chosen basis set to achieve more accurate wavenumber (WN) of the observed normal modes [35,49,50]. Raman intensities have been calculated from the Raman activities (

.

Furthermore, the electronic transition for absorption and emission spectra of the GQDs are explored using time dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) with Coulomb attenuated hybrid exchange–correlation (CAM-B3LYP) [51] functional which includes the effect of long range correction. We have examined the transition among 20 singlet states. All the computations have been performed for the GQDs dissolved in water and without water. For studying the transitions in presence of the solvent, the polarization continuum model (PCM) [52] is used. The absorption and fluorescence spectra have been plotted using Multiwfn software [53]. To evaluate the adsorption energy for gas sensing mechanism we have additionally used WB97XD functional which is reasonably appropriate than B3LYP functional as it correctly takes into account the van-der-Waals interaction among the adsorbate and adsorbent [54].

A 6 × 6 super-cell of graphene sheet has been tailored to obtain 8 different GQDs named as QD-0, QD-1, QD-2, QD-3, QD-4, QD-5, QD-6, QD-7. To acquire optimized structure and to confine it, we have initiated by passivating the dangling bonds with hydrogen atom [16]. The GQDs have achieved global minima which are enlisted in Fig. 1 according to their increasing size. The passivation of bare graphene edges gives stability and forms C-H covalent bonds. All these GQDs have CC sp
2
hybridized bonds. In each structure, the appearance of partial double bond suggests that the π orbital electron can transfer from one site to another resulting in different possible conformation of the structures. From Table 1, we can observe the optimized bond lengths, cohesive energy and point group of the structures. The C=C bond lengths of optimized GQDs are in the range of 1.37–1.45 Å and C-H bond length is 1.08–1.09 Å. All these values are well matched with the experimental results [55,56].

In this segment, we focus on the detailed analysis of the electronic properties which significantly depends on the DOS near the Fermi energy level (
E
F
) of the GQDs. We have evaluated the energy gap of the structures which is equal to the difference between the HOMO and LUMO energy values of the DOS spectra as shown for QD-7 in Fig. 2(a) along with their isosurfaces. The DOS spectra of some of the structures are attached in Fig-S1 of the electronic supplementary information (ESI). It is important to note that the HOMO and LUMO represent π and
π
∗
orbitals respectively. The values of the HOMO-LUMO gap along with their Fermi energy (
E
F
) and work functions (W) (evaluated using Eq. (2)) are enlisted in Table 2. In this context, a comparison between the energy gap values evaluated using B3LYP and HSE06 functionals (in Table S1 in ESI) confirms the accuracy of the obtained results. From Eq. (2) it is clear that the Fermi level lies very close to the middle of the HOMO-LUMO gap. The Fermi energy of all the considered structures lies within the range −3.50 eV to −3.30 eV Table 2.

We have observed from Table 2 that QD-0 exhibits highest HOMO-LUMO gap (6.80 eV) while the gap is lowest for QD-6 (0.07 eV). The study also depicts that QD-2 and QD-4 promptly showcase edge magnetism as opposed to other non-magnetic quantum dots. In the following sub-section we have briefly discussed the emergence of this shape-dependent magnetic nature.

As we are dealing with monolayer graphene, its honeycomb nature can be decomposed into two triangular sublattices say A and B. In case of GQDs, a particular count of edge passivation with H atoms in both the sublattices reveal the spin-splitting as shown in Fig-S2 in ESI. This sort of occurrence of edge-magnetism is recently reported by Han et al. [58] using the technique of determinant quantum Monte-Carlo simulation. We have extended this idea and extracted a thumb rule on how to design a GQD with spin-splitted electronic nature. A detailed structural analysis of 8 considered quantum dots provides two necessary conditions:
(6a)

A
+

where,
N
A
and
N
B
are the number of H atoms passivated in A and B sublattice respectively. Satisfying the above mentioned conditions, we can conclude that the zigzag patterned structures QD-2 and QD-4 highlight the spin splitting behavior which results in two different molecular orbitals—alpha (up spin channel) and beta (down spin channel). The spin density of these two structures are displayed in Fig. 2(b) which shows that the maximum contribution to the effective atomic charge density comes from the edge atoms. For consistency we have invoked this relation to achieve another quantum dot with zigzag geometry, namely QD-8, which shows this exact spin-splitted nature as shown in Fig-S3 in ESI.

We have also investigated the variation of work function (W) to check the possibility of thermionic emission. All GQDs have different values of work functions which are plotted with the energy gap data in Fig. 2(c). The values of W are comparatively high for all the structures. We have observed that QD-2 and QD-6 exhibit the highest (6.86 eV) and lowest (3.63 eV) work function respectively. Furthermore, we have calculated the electrical conductivity ( σ ) of these structures using Eq. (4). The values of the dimensionless quantity ln(
σ
0
/ σ ) are mentioned in Table 2. It is observed that QD-6 has the highest electrical conductivity ratio.

Considering the fact that QD-6 having lowest HOMO-LUMO gap (from Table 2) which shows highest chemical reactivity and QD-7 being the most stable structure (from Table 1), we have chosen these two structures for foreign atoms implementation. Here, for doping we have selected boron (B), aluminum (Al), and nitrogen (N) atoms as they are in the vicinity of carbon (C) atom in periodic table having comparable sizes with carbon atoms. Before doping, we have observed the pristine structure of QD-6 and QD-7 have permanent (4.59 Debye) and zero dipole moment respectively. It can easily be explained by observing that due to the presence of charge anisotropy in the pristine structure of QD-6 it possesses permanent dipole moment [35,59]. Observing the similarity in the nature of electrostatic potential (ESP) (Fig-S4 of ESI) of QD-6 (pristine) and QD-6 (BN), we can confirm that their dipole moments should nearly match with each other. This similarity is also noticed for QD-7 (BN) and QD-7 (AlN). However, there occurs a drastic distortion in the potential surface for QD-6 (AlN) implying enhanced dipole moment. It is to be noted that, the doping sites are selected by satisfying the following four important points (i) edges of the structure should not be doped (ii) doped system should have zero imaginary frequency (iii) after doping the system generates enough dipole moment and (iv) stability of the whole system is achieved. The updated dipole moments of these structures after codoping with BN and AlN are enlisted in Table 4. The direction of the dipole moments are shown in Fig. 3.

Raman spectroscopy is a well-known characterization technique which is non-destructive in nature with high spatial and spectral resolution. For all the structures, the passivation of the dangling bonds with added hydrogen (H) atoms confirms the stability of the systems by providing zero imaginary frequency in their vibrational spectra. Due to this addition, some extra normal modes are introduced. But, for having the lightest mass (for H atom) the effect is negligible and C-H stretching modes occur at very high frequency which do not overlap with the CC stretching modes [35].

Raman spectra contains different bands, each of them represents certain type of vibration of the covalent bonds. The most important feature in the Raman spectra of single layer graphene is the G mode appearing at around 1582 cm−1 which is the only mode occurs from first order Raman scattering process in graphene. The G mode, which corresponds to the inplane vibration of the sp
2
carbon atoms, is associated with the doubly degenerate phonon mode with E
2 g
symmetry. For the strong CC bonding and small mass of C atoms, the G band in sp
2
carbons is characteristic of comparatively higher Raman frequency. Here, we have identified the characterizing G mode as well as the breathing-like mode or B mode i.e. the low frequency mode associated with the out-of-phase contraction and expansion of the whole structure. All these modes for each structure are summarized in Table 5 for incident laser wavelength of 532 nm. We have also observed the modes for incident laser wavelength of 785 nm enlisted in Table S2 of ESI. Comparing the Raman spectra for these two wavelengths, we can presume the enhancement of Raman intensity with decrease in incident laser power. Therefore, to get better idea of the structural fingerprint, low wavelength lasers are more preferred.

The smallest structure QD-0 exhibits the characterizing B mode at 981.43 cm−1 and the G mode at 1592.74 cm−1 which lie close to the experimental values [11]. As the number of atoms increases from QD-0 to QD-6, the frequency of their respective B-modes are continuously getting red shifted. We can also observe that for all the pristine structures the G-mode lies within the frequency range of the experimentally observed G-band for graphene sheet (between 1500–1600 cm−1) [11]. The vibrational spectra of QD-6 and QD-7 along with their doped derivatives are displayed in Fig. 4. From this figure, it can be noted that the BN and AlN codoped structures of both QD-6 and QD-7 showcase relatively high intensity peaks compared to their pristine counterparts. This result indicates that the Raman spectra is very sensitive to the weight of the atoms present at either end of the bond. One interesting fact to be noted that there is no such remarkable shift observed in the B-modes with doping type, however the precise analysis of the G-modes unveils that for BN codoped structure of QD-6, the G-mode is red-shifted by 18.79 cm−1 whereas for AlN codoped structure it is slightly blue-shifted (by 5.86 cm−1) with respect to the pristine structure. For QD-7 the G-mode varies noticeably after doping. It is red-shifted by 57.87 cm−1 and 118.69 cm−1 for BN and AlN doped structure. We have also noted the out-of-phase vibration of the foreign atoms in the doped structures (mentioned in Table 5). The vibrational B and G modes of the GQDs with their Raman spectra are mentioned in Fig-S5 of ESI. From the detailed analysis of the vibrations of the doped structures, another interesting fact is observed i.e. the asymmetric stretching of N atom for all the doped structures is situated at around 1280 cm−1 in the Raman spectra. Zafar et al. [60] and Nguyen et al. [61] have successively reported the Raman analysis of microwave-assisted synthesis of GQDs and N-doped GQDs with the specific Raman mode of N around 1300 cm−1 which is consistent with our numerical result of 1280 cm−1.

In this section, we will explore the characterization of the concerned GQDs through electronic transitions by invoking the machinery of time-dependent density functional theory in both gas phase and using water as a solvent. The reason for choosing water is mainly the small size and hydrophilic nature of the GQDs which make them favorable to be dispersed in water [62] where they can exhibit higher stability in water for several months, making them suitable for electrochemical applications like supercapacitors, flexible electronic products [63]. Water’s absorbance cutoff (190 nm) also lies just outside the UV–Vis spectrum range (200–800 nm), aiding in the analysis of characterization via optical transitions. The most probable transition is noted by the dimensionless oscillator strength (O.S.) given by Eq. (7),

〈
μ
a b
〉
/e, where e is the electronic charge. In this study, from Table 6 we have observed minimum (0.62 Å) and maximum (1.56 Å) average displacement for QD-2 and QD-7 respectively.

Hens, in 2008, clarified that O.S. of a transition starting from the HOMO of a many-electron system can exceed unity [66]. In our cases, we have ‘f’ values around the domain of 0.3–1.3 for absorption phenomena in the presence of water whereas in the fluorescence case, we can observe many probable electronic transitions among different molecular orbitals with ‘f’ ranging from 0.5–2. The highest peak of the spectra for all GQDs mostly lies in the UV region (100–400 nm). All the data recorded from the analysis are enlisted in Tables 6 and 7. As observed from Table 6, the wavelength, O.S. and dipole moment display no significant changes before and after the structures dissolved in water for the pristine. However, for the doped derivative of QD-7, the wavelength is slightly blue-shifted by 82.1 nm and the transition probability increases. Now, from Table 7 the fluorescence spectra gradually showcase red-shift of excitation wavelengths as one adds water to the GQDs. The increase in O.S. can induce more number of electrons participating in the emission. For all the GQDs we have noted down the maximum contribution of the transition components between HOMO-LUMO states with their highest corresponding O.S.

In Fig. 5, the graphs have been depicted for spectra analysis of absorption and emission of QD-7 along with its doped variants both with and without solvent. The UV–Vis spectra of all other structures are attached in Fig-S6 in ESI. Observing the nature of the curves, an overall tendency of the occurrence of emission towards higher wavelength compared to the absorption significantly implies increased transition probability. Another interesting feature we have noticed is that in the case of water-dissolved QDs, the absorbance value for emission significantly increases concerning absorption.

As far as environmental hazards are concerned, detecting the concentration of toxic gases in the surroundings through the new-age solid-state sensors still fascinates current researchers. To design such a device, the chemically inert functionalized graphene surfaces have endeavored to be used [67–69]. Carbon monoxide (CO) is a toxic, flammable gas that is colorless, tasteless, slightly less dense than air and of severe health hazard if inhaled for a long time. Therefore, it is essential to ascertain the trace amount of this harmful gas with reasonable precision. Our approach is guided by the design of the GQDs, choice of dopants, and overall objectives. Among the tailored 8 quantum dots, QD-6 and QD-7 are selected for gas sensing applications for two reasons: (i) Pristine QD-6 exhibits a high electric dipole moment of 4.59 D, while QD-7 is structurally symmetric with nearly zero dipole moment, the highest atom count, and a larger surface area, enabling them to attract toxic, polar gases like CO via dipole–dipole interactions. (ii) We aimed to highlight the impact of hole doping, as opposed to electron doping, on their response to CO. Starting with pristine QDs, N doping was attempted to enhance stability and charge asymmetry but was unsuccessful. Subsequent hole doping with B and Al proved effective, maintaining stability and increasing the electric dipole moment. From Table 4 we have noticed that after the introduction of foreign atoms in both the structures of QD-6 and QD-7, due to the presence of charge anisotropy in the system, there is a significant increase in their electric dipole moments. Thus, QD-6 (N) and QD-7 (N) serve as appropriate reference points (Fig-S7 in ESI), with BN and AlN codoping offering an alternative roadmap for gas sensing. All the above QDs are explored for the possibility of CO sensing with their sensitivity comparison as shown in Fig. 6. From the nature of the ESP of QD-7 (BN codoped) (Fig-S4 in ESI), the absence of potential surface suggests prohibition of the adsorption of gas molecules at that position. Interestingly, the calculations of the magnetic structure QD-8 reveal that the adsorption energy is found to be −8 meV (exothermic) and recovery time comes out to be 1.37 ps.

After a careful observation, it is evident that the clusters have maintained their planar configuration even after the adsorption and no additional bond has been created between the adsorbate and adsorbent atoms. In this physisorption process, we have noticed that in the case of AlN codoped structure, a buckling occurs due to the increase in the size of the Al atom concerning other constituents. It is noticed that the axis of the CO molecule is not exactly parallel to the surface of the adsorbent. From Fig. 6, the CO molecule makes an angle of 3.04° above the surface of the BN codoped QD-6 structure. The optimized height of the C and O atoms of CO molecule is at 3.50 Å and 3.94 Å respectively. Due to this adsorption, there occurs a redistribution of charges in the system (shown in Fig-S8 of ESI), as reflected in the value of dipole moment, compared to the corresponding isolated surface as mentioned in Table 8. These DFT-computed dipole moments of the adsorbed systems are very close to the values of the dipole moments evaluated using the vector sum formula [42] as follows :
(8)
p =

2

p
2

+ 2

c o s θ
Here,
p
1
and
p

are the dipole moments of the adsorbent and CO molecule respectively and θ is the angle between them which has been evaluated from the optimized structure after CO sensing. The values of the electric dipole moments given in Table 8 do not match reasonably well with each other because in case of vector resultant rule, we consider two dipoles to be independent, but in case of physisorption with the existent van-der-Waals interaction among adsorbate and adsorbent must be considered. The adsorption energy (
E
a d
) has been calculated using the following relation [42].
(9)

are the energy of adsorbent GQD, CO molecule and the adsorbed system respectively. The adsorption energy values for all the concerned systems are enlisted in Table 9. The negative sign indicates the success and its consequent exothermic feature of occurrence. As we know the thermal energy at room temperature is roughly 25 meV, the adsorption strengths tabulated in Table 9 suggest that this CO adsorption on these functionalized graphene surfaces can easily be recognized at ambient temperature. Furthermore, we have evaluated the recovery time ( τ ) from the transition state theory [59]:
(10)
τ =
ν
0
− 1

E
a d

B
T
where
ν
0
− 1
(= 10−12 s) is the attempt frequency [42], T is the temperature and
k
B
is the Boltzmann constant. It is clear from Table 9 that pristine, N doped and BN codoped structures possess recovery time of the order of nanoseconds. This denotes that the gas molecule can be sensed and quickly desorbed at room temperature without making any prominent change to the surface. On the other hand, for the AlN codoped structures, significantly larger recovery times of the order of milliseconds and minutes have been observed. This higher recovery time for AlN@GQDs is primarily attributed to the highly exothermic nature of sensing towards a CO gas molecule with higher adsorption energy (−0.89 eV) since Al and N doping induces significant charge redistribution as depicted in the value of electric dipole moment, creating localized charge traps where stronger electrostatic interactions with the adsorbate prolong their retention on the surface. This codoping introduces defects or modifies the existing (or previous) ones which act as binding sites with higher adsorption energy, making desorption slower as given in Table 9. To add more credibility to the results, the subsequent sensor sensitivity (S) can be an important parameter as far as the motif behind the fabrication of a sensing device is concerned. This can be computed using the relation [75]:
(11)
S =
σ
p
−
σ
g
σ
p
× 100 %
where
σ
p
and
σ
g
are the pure substrate’s electrical conductivity and the target gas’s presence, respectively. It has been noted from Table 9 that BN codoped structure of QD-6 possesses the lowest sensitivity (3.22%) and AlN codoped structure of QD-7 bears a significantly high percentage of 92.24%. As pointed out in Table 9, AlN-doped GQDs exhibit lower adsorption energy and shorter recovery time than Al@graphene. The lower adsorption energy suggests that the AlN-doped GQDs surface interactions with adsorbed species (such as gas molecules) are more easily reversible, leading to faster recovery times after detection. The direct synthesis of large-area Al-doped graphene by chemical vapor deposition [76] has stretched the progress of the regime of substitutionally doped graphene family further. Ao et al. have demonstrated [72] how Al-doped graphene surface can enhance the sensitivity of CO adsorption. Recently, Ajeel et al. have ensured the first-principles computation of the effect of AlN dimers on the surface of graphene quantum dot [77]. Our work however highlights how this AlN doping, as opposed to single Al substitution boosts the sensing performance and stands out as a better prospect as the electrical conductivity changes significantly—much higher than the BN codoped counterpart. Before and after the adsorption, we have noticed that the presence of CO significantly changes the HOMO and LUMO energies Table 10. In particular, for all the cases discussed, there is a slight increment in the energy gap post-adsorption of around 10–20 meV. A highest 130 meV increment in the energy gap is observed for AlN doped QD-7, the consequence of which was addressed earlier through sensitivity (S). We have further used the Raman and UV–Vis spectroscopy to check how this sensing affects the characterizing peaks of the original substrate. Firstly, no imaginary frequencies exist in the vibrational spectra after this CO molecule gets attached which establishes the dynamical stability of this physisorption. The Raman active mode of the CO gas molecule consists of only one stretching mode at a wavenumber of 2123.44 cm−1. The consequent shifts in this CO stretching mode due to its adsorption on the doped quantum dots are enlisted in Table 10. The vibrational mode for CO is softened by an amount of 11.48 cm−1, 9.87 cm−1, 54.71 cm−1, 8.46 cm−1 and 32.75 cm−1 for the structures as given sequentially in the table. In addition, the presence of CO molecule significantly reduces the absorbance values in the UV–Vis spectra by 77.6%, 2.8%, 71.19%, 1.86% and 10.11% for the consequent structures mentioned in Table 10 with definite visible changes in the spectra as well. The Raman spectra and UV–Vis spectra of the AlN codoped structure of QD-7 are showcased in Fig. 7. The spectra of the remaining structures showing remarkable changes before and after CO detection are attached in Fig-S9 of ESI. These prominent red-shifts in the vibrational as well as in the optical absorbance spectra excellently display and confirm the presence of CO molecule on the adsorbent surface.

In conclusion, we have meticulously explored the features associated with a graphene sheet with 8 distinct achiral graphene quantum dots revealing diverse range of optoelectronic features with much higher applicability. Vibrational analysis ensured the stability of these systems with zero imaginary frequencies with signature Raman modes present all over the spectra showing structural fingerprints. Pristine GQDs with zigzag edges showcase spin-polarized channels. Electronic transitions are thoroughly explored for 20 singlet excitations with absorption and fluorescence spectra keeping water as a solvent. Comparative analysis depict higher oscillator strengths in the emission spectra with corresponding high transition dipole moments under the presence of solvent. Surface functionalization through codoping of group (III-V) elements such as B-N and Al-N on the most stable quantum dots divulge significant charge anisotropy with decently high dipole moment up to 5.56 Debye. Toxic CO gas has been successfully sensed with high adsorption energy showing physisorption and consequently high sensitivity of around 92% ensuring high promises for futuristic sensor devices.
